If you recall from our earlier courses, we discussed how humans are overwhelmingly influenced by their emotions. By demonstrating empathy, we build trust-based influence. Good negotiators identify and use their counterparts' emotions as a way to build influence with them, while de-escalating their anxiety. The design of the label skill is to either enhance positive emotions or diffuse negative ones. Use phrases like it seems like, it sounds like, it looks like, or it feels like to start your labels. Then finish that phrase with the observation of the emotion or dynamic you've perceived. You can also start labels with, you seem, you sound, you look. Labels encourage your counterpart to be more responsive. Some people have described labels as unlocking the floodgates of truth-telling. These same skills in the law enforcement world, when used on terrorists, has been described as the closest thing to a truth serum that law enforcement has. There's a common theme here, getting people to trust you and tell you important things. We do severely limit the use of the I word. Using I shows them that at that moment you're more interested on yourself than them. It assigns you the personal responsibility of the words that follow and the dynamic they might cause. I is self-centering. Used at the wrong time, it distracts them from the thought processes you want them to engage in. For example, saying, what I'm hearing is, is wrong. Because it makes it more about you in this context. On the other hand, you might need to say, it sounds like I've upset you. With the fugitives in the Harlem apartment, we spotted their feelings, turned them into words, and then very calmly and respectfully repeated their emotions back to them. Think of labeling as a shortcut to intimacy, a time-saving emotional hack, if you will. Now, I bet you're thinking to yourself that the next time you try to label someone's feelings, they're going to fire back with, don't you dare tell me how I feel? Or something of that nature. I'll let you in on a little secret. People never even notice. If you follow these simple guidelines, labeling will become one of the most powerful tools in your arsenal. The first step is to detect the other person's emotional state. If you stand face to face with someone, you'll be able to collect a wealth of information, just from their words to an embodied language. The trick to spotting their feelings is by paying close attention to any changes. The undergoing response to external events or external stimuli, which most of the time are your words. Let me ask you this quick question. Have you ever been to see a psychic before?
